quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Orbital velocity"?

options.text: In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision
v = √(GM/r); the speed needed for a stable circular orbit at distance r from the center of mass M
A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work
A system on which no net external force acts; internal forces cancel by Newton's Third Law

quiz_questions.explanation: "Orbital velocity" is defined as: v = √(GM/r); the speed needed for a stable circular orbit at distance r from the center of mass M. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Kepler's Third Law"?

options.text: 1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
r⊥ = r sinθ; the perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force
T² = (4π²/GM)r³; orbital period squared is proportional to orbital radius cubed
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Kepler's Third Law" is defined as: T² = (4π²/GM)r³; orbital period squared is proportional to orbital radius cubed. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Escape velocity"?

options.text: a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt²; the time derivative of velocity, indicating how quickly velocity changes
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum
v_esc = √(2GM/r); the minimum speed needed to escape a gravitational field entirely (total energy = 0)
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: "Escape velocity" is defined as: v_esc = √(2GM/r); the minimum speed needed to escape a gravitational field entirely (total energy = 0). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Geosynchronous orbit"?

options.text: ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs
An orbit with T = 24 hours at r ≈ 42,200 km from Earth's center; the satellite stays above one point on the equator
ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0; conditions for an object to remain at rest with no linear or rotational acceleration
v_cm = Σm_i v_i / M_total = p_total / M_total; constant for an isolated system

quiz_questions.explanation: "Geosynchronous orbit" is defined as: An orbit with T = 24 hours at r ≈ 42,200 km from Earth's center; the satellite stays above one point on the equator. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Total orbital energy"?

options.text: E = KE + PE = -GMm/(2r); negative for bound orbits; more negative means a lower, tighter orbit
U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium
A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force
The net impulse on an object equals its change in momentum: J_net = p_final - p_initial

quiz_questions.explanation: "Total orbital energy" is defined as: E = KE + PE = -GMm/(2r); negative for bound orbits; more negative means a lower, tighter orbit. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v = √(GM/r); the speed needed for a stable circular orbit at distance r from the center of mass M"?

options.text: Conservation of angular momentum
Orbital velocity
Gravitational PE (near Earth)
Angular impulse

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Orbital velocity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "T² = (4π²/GM)r³; orbital period squared is proportional to orbital radius cubed"?

options.text: Kepler's Third Law
Binding energy
Perfectly inelastic collision
Power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Kepler's Third Law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v_esc = √(2GM/r); the minimum speed needed to escape a gravitational field entirely (total energy = 0)"?

options.text: Weight
Perfectly inelastic collision
Energy dissipation
Escape velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Escape velocity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An orbit with T = 24 hours at r ≈ 42,200 km from Earth's center; the satellite stays above one point on the equator"?

options.text: Escape condition
Geosynchronous orbit
Free-body diagram
Common moments of inertia

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Geosynchronous orbit".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E = KE + PE = -GMm/(2r); negative for bound orbits; more negative means a lower, tighter orbit"?

options.text: Work-Energy Theorem
Parallel axis theorem
Total orbital energy
Torque-angular momentum relation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Total orbital energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Orbital Motion is accurate?

options.text: Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
For an isolated system, the center of mass velocity is constant regardless of internal interactions, making it invaluable for analyzing explosions and multi-body systems.
The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.
Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Orbital Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Orbital Motion?

options.text: The physical (compound) pendulum accounts for distributed mass: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)), where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot and d is the distance from pivot to center of mass.
Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM), which is Kepler's Third Law.
This formula (with U = 0 at r = ∞) replaces the approximate U = mgh valid only near Earth's surface.
Both approximate SHM for small oscillations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Orbital Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Orbital Motion?

options.text: Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
Geosynchronous orbits, escape velocity (v_esc = √(2GM/r)), and energy in orbits (E = -GMm/2r) are key applications of gravitational and energy concepts.
Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv) and represents the quantity of motion of an object.
Power and Efficiency describe the rate of energy transfer and the fraction of input energy converted to useful output.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Orbital Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Orbital Motion?

options.text: Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.
The relationship between linear and angular quantities (v = rω, a_t = rα, a_c = rω²) connects translational and rotational motion.
When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Orbital Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Orbital Motion?

options.text: The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.
Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM), which is Kepler's Third Law.
Conservative forces (gravity, springs) have associated potential energy functions, while non-conservative forces (friction) convert mechanical energy to thermal energy.
A mass on a spring executing SHM with ω = √(k/m) demonstrates how periodic oscillations at a source generate traveling waves when coupled to a medium.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Orbital Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Orbital Motion?

options.text: Earthquake engineering analyzing seismic wave propagation (P-waves longitudinal, S-waves transverse) through different geological layers
Elevator engineering calculating cable tension and motor force requirements for different loading conditions using F = ma for the elevator-passenger system
Rocket propulsion using F = dp/dt with changing mass: thrust = (dm/dt)·v_exhaust, relating exhaust velocity and fuel burn rate to propulsive force
SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Orbital Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Orbital Motion?

options.text: Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region
Musical instrument design: string tension and density determine wave speed and thus the fundamental frequency of vibrating strings
Automotive engineering placing a vehicle's center of mass low and centrally to optimize handling, stability, and rollover resistance
Ice skater spins: decreasing I by pulling arms in causes ω to increase to conserve L; extending arms slows the spin

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Orbital Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Orbital Motion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Interplanetary mission design computing escape velocity from Earth and transfer orbits (Hohmann transfers) to reach Mars or other planets

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Orbital Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Orbital Motion?

options.text: SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Spin cycle in washing machines using centripetal force to push water outward through drum holes for efficient drying
Earthquake engineering analyzing seismic wave propagation (P-waves longitudinal, S-waves transverse) through different geological layers
Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Orbital Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Orbital Motion in practice?

options.text: Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input
Wind turbine design optimizing blade angular velocity to maximize energy capture while maintaining safe centripetal acceleration at blade tips
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Orbital Motion.
